pi_items.source: Air monitoring report (air_monitoring_report.txt line 28); OSHA Annotated Table Z-2

pi_items.metadata_value: PERC (38 ppm vs PEL 100 ppm — within limit)
pi_items.verified_value: Tetrachloroethylene (PERC) measured at 38 ppm; OSHA PEL is 100 ppm (8-hour TWA), so 38 ppm is within regulatory limit
pi_items.source: Air monitoring report (air_monitoring_report.txt line 33); OSHA Annotated Table Z-2

pi_items.verified_value: n-Hexane is the chemical in Zone 4 where a worker reported peripheral neuropathy symptoms (numbness, weakness, grip strength loss). n-Hexane is scientifically documented as causing peripheral neuropathy through occupational exposure.
pi_items.source: Air monitoring report (air_monitoring_report.txt lines 16-18); NCBI StatPearls n-Hexane Toxicity article; NIOSH and CDC documentation

pi_items.metadata_value: n-Hexane metabolized to 2,5-hexanedione which causes peripheral neuropathy
pi_items.verified_value: n-Hexane is metabolized in the liver to 2,5-hexanedione, which forms covalent cross-links with neurofilament proteins, disrupting axonal transport and causing axonal swelling and nerve degeneration, particularly in peripheral nerves. This causes peripheral neuropathy characterized by numbness, weakness, and potential paralysis.
pi_items.source: NCBI Bookshelf: n-Hexane Toxicity (StatPearls); CDC NIOSH Pocket Guide to Chemical Hazards; Multiple peer-reviewed studies on hexane neuropathy mechanism

privileged_info_summary: The task involves identifying OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) exceedances from air monitoring data at a manufacturing facility. All measured concentrations and PEL values are verified against authoritative sources: Xylene at 115 ppm exceeds the 100 ppm PEL; MEK at 255 ppm exceeds the 200 ppm PEL; n-Hexane at 680 ppm exceeds the 500 ppm PEL; Methanol at 400 ppm exceeds the 200 ppm PEL. Conversely, Toluene at 185 ppm and PERC at 38 ppm are within their respective PELs of 200 ppm and 100 ppm. The worker in Zone 4 exhibits peripheral neuropathy symptoms, which is medically documented as caused by n-Hexane exposure. n-Hexane is metabolized to the neurotoxic compound 2,5-hexanedione, which disrupts neurofilament function and causes peripheral nerve damage. All data sourcing and regulatory values derive from real OSHA standards and NIOSH guidelines.
